description: Economics and finance - derivatives, game theory, monetary policy, markets.

distractors: prices follow a normal distribution symmetrically
volatility changes dynamically with price level
discrete time jumps between price observations

facts.context: Moral hazard in insurance occurs when:
facts.truth: insured party takes more risk after getting coverage
distractors: the insurer possesses hidden information about risk
insurance premiums are set at unreasonably high levels
all insurance claims are discovered to be fraudulent

facts.context: A Nash equilibrium is reached when:
facts.truth: no player benefits from unilateral deviation
distractors: all players simultaneously achieve maximum payoffs
players cooperate to reach socially optimal outcome
the game has exactly one unique solution possible

facts.context: Quantitative easing by central banks involves:
facts.truth: buying assets to increase the money supply
distractors: raising interest rates to control inflation directly
selling government bonds to reduce liquidity
printing physical currency notes for distribution

facts.context: Adverse selection in markets occurs when:
distractors: all market participants have identical information
markets always clear at the efficient price
prices perfectly reflect fundamental value always

facts.truth: call, put, stock, and risk-free bond prices
distractors: only call options to stock prices directly
option prices to historical volatility only
futures prices to spot prices exclusively

facts.context: In the prisoner's dilemma, the Nash equilibrium is:
facts.truth: both defect despite mutual cooperation being better
distractors: both cooperate achieving the optimal outcome
one cooperates while the other defects randomly
no equilibrium exists in this game structure

facts.context: When central bank raises interest rates, typically:
distractors: borrowing increases due to higher returns available
currency weakens against foreign currencies
inflation immediately increases in the economy

facts.context: The efficient market hypothesis (EMH) strong form claims:
facts.truth: prices reflect all information including private
distractors: markets are always inefficient and exploitable
only public information is reflected in prices
technical analysis can consistently beat markets

facts.context: Delta hedging an option position requires:
facts.truth: holding shares equal to option's delta
distractors: buying equal numbers of calls and puts always
holding the underlying until option expires
no adjustment as markets move over time

facts.truth: no one can improve without making another worse off
distractors: everyone has exactly equal amounts of resources
total welfare is maximized for all participants
the allocation was determined by market prices

facts.context: An inverted yield curve (short rates > long rates) often signals:
distractors: strong economic growth and expansion ahead
stable economic conditions with no change expected
high inflation expectations in the near term
